entry.title: Re: Release strategy for pricing condition record (MEK1,...
entry.content: Standard SAP does not trigger release strategy for condition record changes (MEK1/MEK2). The recommended approach is to implement a custom workflow or BRF+ rule to govern condition maintenance. Direct integration with PO release strategy is not supported.  You can explore a custom enhancement to monitor condition changes and trigger an approval process if required.

entry.title: Re: How to copy roh parts from one super bom to another w...
entry.content: To copy ROH parts from one super BOM to another with all dependencies, use CEWB or BOM Copy functions. For dependencies (object dependencies, classifications, documents), you’ll need to either configure CEWB to include them or build a custom program using BOM and dependency BAPIs.

entry.title: Re: Issue with release strategy
entry.content:
Which system you have done release strategy configuration set up and which system you are testing - share those details!

NOTE: In case of release strategy set up done in development system and TR transport to quality system- check release characteristics and release class created in quality system along with value object/s are mapped in CL20N for your release strategy and release class before testing in quality system

entry.title: Re: Is it possible to remove page number in Time Form PDF
entry.content: As per SAP KBA 3532347, Time Statement Forms follow standard system design and do not support layout customization. Since page numbering is part of the standard footer, it cannot be modified or removed.

entry.title: Re: How to set up combine Multiple Sales Schedule Agreeme...
entry.content:
Sales Order Schedule Lines app is designed for processing schedule lines per agreement. It does not support combining multiple Sales Schedule Agreements into one delivery. Each agreement is treated independently, even if the customer master has “Order Combination” flagged. That flag only influences delivery creation when using the delivery creation apps, not the schedule line processing app.
Having said that, in Public Cloud, combining multiple Sales Schedule Agreements into one delivery is only supported via the Create Outbound Delivery with Order Reference app.

entry.title: Re: Eligible earnings for salaried and hourly employees case
entry.content:
Although not clearly stated, I will answer from the standpoint of Variable Pay and using eligible earnings as a basis for the bonus.   In this case, the earnings are loaded via a csv file and the source does not matter.  If you are trying to map data from two different sources into one field on the salary template or a variable pay template, it cannot be done.   You would have to use the MDF for everyone and map the data from one field in the MDF into the single field on the template.

If you are trying to use earnings for salary increases, it does not make sense because of the way the schedules vary from week to week including overtime.

As I have mentioned in previous answers to questions, you need to determine what the client is trying to accomplish and look for the simplest way to accomplish.  Always do your best to consult with the client to help them simply the process whenever possible.

entry.title: [UTILITIES] [OIL, GAS & ENERGY] Learning that enables transformation – what Energy & Utilities need
entry.content:
Utilities, and Oil, Gas & Energy industries are undergoing rapid and far‑reaching transformation. New technologies, regulatory pressure, sustainability targets, and evolving business models are reshaping organizations at every level.

But there is a question we often underestimate:

Is learning evolving enough to keep up with transformation?

At EUC2026, we will open this discussion in the upcoming session
“Learning that enables transformation: What energy and utilities need next”,
taking place on Thursday, 23 April.

This session is intended to trigger reflection, exchange, and honest discussion on how learning must evolve to truly support SAP‑driven transformation in our industries.

Before we continue, it’s worth pausing on a few uncomfortable but important reflections:

  • Do transformation programs often fail not because of technology, but because skills are missing or arrive too late?
  • Is learning still too often treated as an afterthought, instead of being embedded into projects from day one?
  • Do standard curricula rarely reflect the reality of day‑to‑day operations in Utilities or Oil, Gas & Energy?
  • Are roles changing faster than job descriptions, and is learning struggling to keep up?

If any of this resonates, the session and the conversation is for you.

What we will explore together

During the session, we will look at:

  • Learning as a critical enabler of successful transformation in Energy & Utilities
  • SAP learning platforms and methods available for customers and partners
  • Role‑ and solution‑based learning content for Utilities and Oil, Gas & Energy
  • Learning formats that fit real projects and daily operations
  • Where SAP Learning is heading next
  • An open exchange on customer and partner learning needs

The real value, however, will come from the conversation, not the slides.

Start the conversation now

To make the session as relevant and practical as possible, we want to start collecting input already now.

Whether you are attending EUC2026 in person or following the post from outside, we invite you to share:

  • Questions you would like to see addressed during this or later sessions
  • Challenges you face when upskilling teams during transformations
  • Gaps you see in today’s Utilities or Oil, Gas & Energy learning offerings
  • Ideas for learning formats that actually work in your projects and operations

Your input will directly shape the discussion during the session and further follow-ups. 

Learning that evolves with the industry

Transformation is continuous — and learning must be continuous as well.

What our industries increasingly need is learning that:

  • Evolves with SAP innovation and industry priorities
  • Is role‑based, practical, and scenario‑driven
  • Supports both large transformation programs and daily operations
  • Is co‑created together with customers and partners

The EUC2026 session is just a starting point in that journey.

Questions to think about and comment on

To kick off the discussion, consider these questions and feel free to share your thoughts under the blog post right now:

  • When was learning last truly critical to the success of one of your projects?
  • Where do today’s learning offerings fall short for Utilities or Oil, Gas & Energy?
  • Which roles in your organization are most affected by missing or outdated skills?
  • Do your teams need more depth, more speed, or more flexibility in learning?
  • How should learning be embedded into transformation projects from the start?
  • What would “learning that truly enables transformation” look like for you?

Stay connected: join the SAP Industries Learning Group

To continue this exchange beyond the session and beyond EUC2026, we invite you to join the SAP Industries Learning Group.

The group is a space to:

  • Exchange experiences and challenges openly
  • Share feedback, questions, and ideas
  • Stay informed about learning developments for Utilities, and Oil, Gas & Energy and all other industries
  • Help shape the future learning curriculum together

👉Join the group, stay connected, and be part of shaping what learning needs to enable next.

We’re looking forward to the discussion on Thursday, 23 April on the EUC2026 and to the conversation right now under the blog post. 

Utilities Oil, Gas, and Energy

entry.title: Re: Batch invoicing job for delivery plus sales order wit...
entry.content:
Your Processing logic should be

  • Step 1: Select eligible records from VKDFS based on Sales Org (VKDFS-VKORG).
  • Step 2: WBS Validation
    • Check if VKDFS-PS_PSP_PNR (internal WBS element number) is not initial.
    • Validate against Project System table PRPS to ensure the WBS element is:
      • Released (PRPS-STAT status = REL),
      • Not locked (PRPS-LOEKZ ≠ X),
      • Active in controlling.
    • If WBS is invalid, exclude the record and log for manual review.
  • Step 3: Group records by Sales Order (VKDFS-VBELN) + Delivery (VKDFS-VBELV).
  • Step 4: Trigger billing creation for each group (via Schedule Billing Creation app, enhanced with BAdI SD_BILLING_DOCUMENT_CREATE).
  • Step 5: Ensure one billing document per group.
  • Step 6: Update billing output type to EDI immediately.

entry.title: How to Integrate UI5 Test Coverage with SonarQube in SAP UI5 Projects
entry.content:
When working with SAP UI5 applications, automated tests are already a good practice. But once the project grows, test execution alone is no longer enough. Teams want to see how much of the application is actually covered by tests, especially in pull requests and CI/CD pipelines.

In this post, I will show how I implemented test coverage reporting for a SAP UI5 application and integrated it with SonarQube so that coverage metrics are visible directly in the quality dashboard.

The solution is based on:

  • ui5-test-runner for running QUnit tests with coverage enabled
  • nyc for generating LCOV reports
  • a custom normalization step to include untested files
  • SonarQube for importing and displaying coverage metrics

This setup works especially well in CI pipelines and helps teams track both coverage on new code and overall project coverage.

Why This Is Needed

In many UI5 projects, unit tests exist, but coverage is not published anywhere centrally. As a result:

  • developers do not see how well new changes are covered
  • pull requests cannot be validated against coverage quality gates
  • SonarQube shows no meaningful UI coverage data
  • uncovered source files may be silently ignored

The main challenge is that in UI5 projects, coverage collection is not always straightforward when tests are executed in the browser. Even if coverage is enabled, the generated report may contain only files that were actually loaded during test execution. This creates an incomplete picture for SonarQube.

To solve this, I implemented an approach that not only generates LCOV coverage but also normalizes it so that all relevant source files are represented, including files with 0% coverage.

Solution Overview

The final flow looks like this:

  1. ui5-test-runner runs QUnit tests with coverage instrumentation
  2. nyc generates the LCOV report
  3. a custom script normalizes the report and adds missing source files
  4. SonarQube imports the LCOV file and displays the coverage metrics

This gives a reliable and CI-friendly setup for SAP UI5 applications.

Prerequisites

Install the required development dependencies:

npm install --save-dev ui5-test-runner nyc puppeteer

What each package is used for

Package Purpose
ui5-test-runnerRuns UI5/QUnit tests and supports code coverage
nycIstanbul-based coverage engine used under the hood
puppeteerRuns tests in headless Chrome, useful for CI

Step 1. Configure ui5-test-runner

Create a ui5-test-runner.json file with the basic setup:

{
 "pageTimeout": 60000,
 "coverage": true,
 "coverageReportDir": "coverage",
 "reportDir": "report",
 "parallel": 2
}

What this configuration does

  • enables coverage collection
  • stores coverage artifacts in the coverage folder
  • defines a timeout for browser-based execution
  • allows parallel test execution for better performance

Step 2. Configure NYC

Even though ui5-test-runner handles much of the work, it relies on NYC internally.
That is why adding an explicit .nycrc.json file is important.

{
 "all": true,
 "sourceMap": false,
 "reporter": ["lcov", "text-summary"],
 "report-dir": "./coverage",
 "temp-dir": "./.nyc_output",
 "include": ["webapp/**/*.js"],
 "exclude": ["webapp/test/**", "webapp/localService/**"],
 "cwd": "."
}

Important points here

  • all: true is important because we want full project visibility
  • test sources are excluded from business code coverage
  • lcov is required because this is the format SonarQube imports

Step 3. Configure SonarQube

Now we need to tell SonarQube where the UI5 sources, tests, and LCOV report are located.

Create or update sonar-project.properties:

 

 
sonar.projectKey=your-project-key
sonar.projectName=your-project-name

# Source configuration
sonar.sources=webapp/
sonar.exclusions=webapp/localService/**,**/node_modules/**

# Test configuration
sonar.tests=webapp/test/
sonar.test.inclusions=webapp/test/**/*.js

# Coverage report path
sonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths=coverage/lcov.info
sonar.coverage.exclusions=webapp/test/**,webapp/localService/**

# Encoding
sonar.sourceEncoding=UTF-8

At this point, the most important property is:

sonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths=coverage/lcov.info

This is the file SonarQube reads to compute the coverage metrics.

Step 4. Add NPM Scripts

The next step is to automate local and CI execution.

In package.json:

{
"scripts": {
"test": "ui5 serve --config ui5-test.yaml --port 8080 & SERVER_PID=$!; sleep 3; ui5-test-runner --url http://localhost:8080/test/unit/unitTests.qunit.html --coverage --coverage-proxy; TEST_EXIT=$?; kill $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null || true; exit $TEST_EXIT",
"test:ci": "ui5 serve --config ui5-test.yaml --port 8080 & SERVER_PID=$!; sleep 3; ui5-test-runner --url http://localhost:8080/test/unit/unitTests.qunit.html --coverage --coverage-proxy --ci; TEST_EXIT=$?; kill $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null || true; npm run coverage:normalize; exit $TEST_EXIT",
"coverage:normalize": "node scripts/normalize-coverage.js"
}
}

Why --coverage-proxy is important

When UI5 tests are executed through a served application URL, --coverage-proxy enables on-the-fly instrumentation. Without it, coverage may be missing or incomplete.

Key flags

Flag Meaning
--coverageEnables coverage collection
--coverage-proxyInstruments files dynamically in browser-based execution
--ciUses CI-friendly non-interactive output

Step 5. Normalize Coverage for Untested Files

This was the most important part of the implementation.

The problem

When --coverage-proxy is used, only files that are actually loaded during the test run are instrumented.
That means if a source file is never loaded, it may not appear in lcov.info at all.

For SonarQube, this is problematic because missing files are not always treated the same way as explicitly uncovered files. As a result, the reported coverage can be misleading.

The fix

I added a normalization script that:

  1. scans all source files in webapp
  2. checks which files are already present in lcov.info
  3. adds missing source files with 0% coverage
  4. normalizes paths so that they match SonarQube expectations

Create scripts/normalize-coverage.js:

const fs = require('fs');
const path = require('path');

const lcovPath = 'coverage/lcov.info';
const webappDir = 'webapp';

// Get all JS source files (excluding test and localService)
function getAllSourceFiles() {
const files = [];
function walk(dir) {
const entries = fs.readdirSync(dir, { withFileTypes: true });
for (const entry of entries) {
const fullPath = path.join(dir, entry.name);
if (entry.isDirectory()) {
if (entry.name !== 'test' && entry.name !== 'localService' && entry.name !== 'node_modules') {
walk(fullPath);
}
} else if (entry.name.endsWith('.js')) {
const relativePath = path.relative(webappDir, fullPath).replace(/\\/g, '/');
files.push(relativePath);
}
}
}
walk(webappDir);
return files;
}

// Parse existing lcov.info to get covered files
function parseLcov(content) {
const covered = new Set();
for (const line of content.split('\n')) {
if (line.startsWith('SF:')) {
covered.add(line.slice(3));
}
}
return covered;
}

// Generate lcov entry for uncovered file
function generateUncoveredEntry(filePath) {
const fullPath = path.join(webappDir, filePath);
if (!fs.existsSync(fullPath)) return null;

const content = fs.readFileSync(fullPath, 'utf8');
const lines = content.split('\n');
const executableLines = [];

for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) {
const line = lines[i].trim();
if (line &&
!line.startsWith('//') &&
!line.startsWith('/*') &&
!line.startsWith('*') &&
line !== '{' &&
line !== '}' &&
line !== '});' &&
line !== ']);' &&
line !== '};' &&
line !== '"use strict";') {
executableLines.push(i + 1);
}
}

if (executableLines.length === 0) return null;

let entry = `SF:${filePath}\n`;
entry += `FNF:0\nFNH:0\n`;
for (const lineNum of executableLines) {
entry += `DA:${lineNum},0\n`;
}
entry += `LF:${executableLines.length}\nLH:0\n`;
entry += `BRF:0\nBRH:0\n`;
entry += `end_of_record\n`;

return entry;
}

// Main
if (!fs.existsSync(lcovPath)) {
console.log('No lcov.info found, skipping');
process.exit(0);
}

let content = fs.readFileSync(lcovPath, 'utf8');
const lines = content.split('\n');
const normalized = [];

// Normalize paths (remove webapp/ prefix to match sonar.sources)
for (const line of lines) {
if (line.startsWith('SF:')) {
let filePath = line.slice(3);
const webappMatch = filePath.match(/[/\\]?webapp[/\\](.+)$/);
if (webappMatch) {
filePath = webappMatch[1];
}
filePath = filePath.replace(/\\/g, '/').replace(/^\//, '');
normalized.push('SF:' + filePath);
} else {
normalized.push(line);
}
}

// Get already covered files
const coveredFiles = parseLcov(normalized.join('\n'));

// Add uncovered files
const allFiles = getAllSourceFiles();
let uncoveredContent = '';

for (const file of allFiles) {
if (!coveredFiles.has(file)) {
const entry = generateUncoveredEntry(file);
if (entry) uncoveredContent += entry;
}
}

fs.writeFileSync(lcovPath, normalized.join('\n') + '\n' + uncoveredContent);
console.log('Coverage normalized for SonarQube');

 

A Critical Detail: Path Mapping Between LCOV and SonarQube

This part is easy to overlook, but it is often the real reason why SonarQube shows 0% coverage even when the LCOV file exists.

If SonarQube is configured with:

sonar.sources=webapp/

then SonarQube treats webapp/ as the root of the source tree.

That means:

  • webapp/model/messages.js is indexed by SonarQube as model/messages.js
  • therefore lcov.info must contain:

 

 
SF:model/messages.js
and not:
 
SF:webapp/model/messages.js

In other words

The path in SF: must be relative to sonar.sources.

That is exactly why the normalization script rewrites the paths.

CI/CD Integration

Once the local setup works, integrating it into the pipeline is straightforward.

SAP Piper example

In .pipeline/config.yml:

steps:
 npmExecuteScripts:
  install: true
  virtualFrameBuffer: false
  dockerImage: 'cypress/browsers:node-20.18.0-chrome-130.0.6723.69-1-ff-131.0.3-edge-130.0.2849.52-1'
  runScripts:
   - 'test:ci'
  sonarExecuteScan:
  serverUrl: 'https://sonar.tools.sap'

stages:
 Build:
  npmExecuteScripts: true
  sonarExecuteScan: true

GitHub Actions example

- name: Run tests with coverage
run: npm run test:ci

- name: SonarQube Scan
uses: sonarqube-scan-action@v1
with:
args: >
-Dsonar.projectKey=${{ env.PROJECT_KEY }}

Generated Coverage Artifacts

After running:

npm run test:ci

 

you should see:

coverage/
├── lcov.info
├── lcov-report/
│ └── index.html
└── cobertura-coverage.xml

 

What these files are used for

  • lcov.info → imported by SonarQube
  • lcov-report/ → human-readable HTML report for local inspection
  • cobertura-coverage.xml → optional integration with other tools

How Coverage Appears in SonarQube

After the scan, SonarQube usually shows two metrics that are especially important:

Metric Meaning
Coverage on New CodeCoverage only for changed lines in the branch or PR
Overall CoverageCoverage across the entire project

This distinction matters a lot.

A project can have:

  • high coverage on new code, because the changed lines were properly tested
  • low overall coverage, because legacy parts of the application still have poor test coverage

That is not a contradiction. It is normal SonarQube behavior.

Troubleshooting

SonarQube shows 0% coverage

Check the following first:

  1. coverage/lcov.info is generated successfully
  2. sonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths points to the correct file
  3. SF: entries match the structure expected by sonar.sources

Useful check:

grep "^SF:" coverage/lcov.info

Some files are missing from coverage

This usually happens because only loaded files were instrumented during the browser run.

That is exactly why the normalization step is needed.

Without normalization, SonarQube may import only a partial coverage picture.

Coverage on new code is high, but overall coverage is low

This is expected and does not necessarily indicate a problem.

It simply means:

  • your recent changes are well tested
  • older application code still lacks coverage

Warning: “Unable to process all coverage”

This may appear when using ui5-test-runner with --coverage-proxy.

In practice, this warning means that not all source files could be discovered automatically during runtime instrumentation.

The custom normalization script compensates for this by adding the missing files manually.

Final Thoughts

For me, the key goal of this implementation was not just to execute UI5 tests with coverage enabled, but to make the results usable and trustworthy inside SonarQube.

The most important lessons from this setup were:

  • generating LCOV is only the first step
  • path alignment between LCOV and sonar.sources is critical
  • browser-based runtime instrumentation does not guarantee full project visibility
  • normalizing missing files makes the SonarQube result much more accurate

With this approach in place, SAP UI5 projects can benefit from the same quality visibility that backend projects usually already have: clear coverage reports, PR metrics, and enforceable quality gates.

References

  • ui5-test-runner Documentation
  • SonarQube JavaScript Coverage Documentation
  • LCOV Format Specification

entry.title: Adjusting an existing Datasphere connection without affecting imported remote tables
entry.content:
Hi,

Could you help me with this question? We have an established connection via Generic JDBC between Datasphere and CRM that uses a PostgreSQL driver (org.postgresql.Driver with URL jdbc:postgresql:). The Postgres database was recently updated to a new version, and I adjusted that same connection. It's the same port, same tables, so I only adjusted the IP address and the new username and password. However, the existing remote tables, which were already imported from that connection, stopped replicating.

I tried the following:

- Creating a new test connection and importing a test table, and it replicates without problems.

- From the existing (and adjusted) connection, I imported another table, and it replicates without problems.

- I also tried stopping automatic replication, changing the source, and reconnecting to the same connection, and updating replication, but without success.

Apparently, the error is only... For existing remote tables originating from that configured source, the error message displayed is as follows:

Error: {"timestamp":"2026-04-21 11:10:30.7592660", "origin":"_sys_task", "message":"internal error: DPAdapterResultSet::Fetch:Camel execution failed. at ptime/query/plan_executor/dml/hex/qe_hex_search.cc:512; Statement: ,INSERT INTO \"XXXXX\".\"XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_CT\" (\"idprevisaovendas\", \"idparceiro\", \"idsafra\", \"idtabeladepreco\", \"idusuario\", \"idlocal\", \"idgrupoempresarial\", \"datacadastro\", \"idfilial\", \"integrado\", \"idcotacaoptax\", \"valorcotacaoptax\", \"idpvmoeda\", \"idpvtabelapreco\", \"valorcotacao\", \"chaveerp\", \"ano\") SELECT \"idprevisaovendas\", \"idparceiro\", \"idsafra\", \"idtabeladepreco\", \"idusuario\", \"idlocal\", \"idgrupoempresarial\", \"datacadastro\", \"idfilial\", \"integrado\", \"idcotacaoptax\", \"valorcotacaoptax\", \"idpvmoeda\", \"idpvtabelapreco\", \"valorcotacao\", \"chaveerp\", \"ano\" FROM (WITH _SYS_WITH_1 AS (SELECT \"idprevisaovendas\" , \"idparceiro\" , \"idsafra\" , \"idtabeladepreco\" , \"idusuario\" , \"idlocal\" , \"idgrupoempresarial\" , \"datacadastro\" , \"idfilial\" , \"integrado\" , \"idcotacaoptax\" , \"valorcotacaoptax\" , \"idpvmoeda\" , \"idpvtabelapreco\" , \"valorcotacao\" , \"chaveerp\" , \"ano\" FROM \"XXXXX\".\"XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT\" AS \"XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT\") , _SYS_WITH_0 AS (SELECT \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT_SRC\".\"idprevisaovendas\" AS \"idprevisaovendas\", \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT_SRC\".\"idparceiro\" AS \"idparceiro\", \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT_SRC\".\"idsafra\" AS \"idsafra\", \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT_SRC\".\"idtabeladepreco\" AS \"idtabeladepreco\", \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT_SRC\".\"idusuario\" AS \"idusuario\", \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT_SRC\".\"idlocal\" AS \"idlocal\", \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT_SRC\".\"idgrupoempresarial\" AS \"idgrupoempresarial\", \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT_SRC\".\"datacadastro\" AS \"datacadastro\", \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT_SRC\".\"idfilial\" AS \"idfilial\", \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public.previsaovendas_VT_SRC\".\"integrado\" AS \"integrado\", \"XXXXX_XXXXX_CRM_XXXXX::public."} 

The goal is to avoid creating a new connection, as that would require re-importing all the remote tables. I already have views and models created using those tables, and I'm unsure how this would impact things. Is it possible to avoid creating a new connection?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Best regards!

entry.title: RBP permission - Access to Non-secured objects for non admin users?
entry.content:
Hi,

We are currently reviewing the roles that we have in our production instance. I've noticed that the permission "Access to non-secured objects", which is an administrator permission, has been enabled in several HR roles, like Data review / I-9 / E-verify roles for in the onboarding process for HR, HRSSC role which is Employee centrale module local admin to manage employee files, etc,

I'm wondering if this permission is necessary for HR to perform their tasks and duties, especially in Onboarding (2.0) and Employee centrale?

Can you please provide insights on this point? Even though my researches show that this permission is not necessary for non system admin HR, I wonder why our implementation partner has enabled it for them. 

We are considering to open up Manage data transaction for certain HRIS users in the countries, but combined with this Access to non-secure objects permission, this would grant them far too much access to system configurations.

Thanks.

Regards,
Weiyuan

entry.title: Re: Need help for Data Protection and Privacy SAP Enable Now
entry.content:
Hello,

 

to achieve this you need to use the Producer. This is not working in Manager.

In Producer:

  1. start editing the "consent" 
  2. right click on it and select "Open folder"
  3. on folder level duplicate the file 1033.txt or copy your modified file there
  4. select the menu Workspace -> "refresh Workspace and all objects" 
  5. Finish editing of the object "consent"
  6. You can than check in Manager, that the new file is visible under Assets with "show streams"

Best regards,

Attila

entry.title: Machine Telepathy - How Latent Memory Links Agents Without Words
entry.content:
Multi-agent systems are increasingly used for complex enterprise tasks. Yet, they still communicate mostly the way humans do: by translating internal reasoning into human language and asking other agents to reconstruct meaning from it. This works, but it is also lossy, ambiguous, and expensive.

As agents become more specialized and workflows more distributed, the next bottleneck is no longer single-model capability, but how computational state itself is shared, preserved, and handed off. Latent memory points to a new paradigm in which agents exchange not only words but richer internal representations, potentially changing both the economics and the capabilities of multi-agent collaboration.

In this deep-dive article based on cutting-edge research, we break down how agents coordinate today, why latent memory is gaining traction, how it could help improve enterprise agent systems, and the key challenges that remain.

Note: This article assumes you are familiar with the fundamentals of AI agents. It is part of our article series on agentic memory that provides the bigger picture: see What is Agentic Memory?  for a definition of agentic memory, and Agentic Memory Unpacked to understand different memory families (parametric, latent, and token-level memory) and types (factual, experiential, and working memory) on a high level.

 

How do agents coordinate?

Currently, agents coordinate through a mix of communication, shared memory, protocol layers, and environment-mediated state. In all of these cases, an agent’s internal reasoning is turned into an explicit representation that other agents can access, interpret, or build on. These representations vary in structure and how they are exchanged, in particular:


Forms of representation and mechanisms used to coordinate multi-agent systems 

 Coordination MechanismExampleStrengthsLimitations
Free-form textMessage passingChat turnsFlexibleVerbose, lossy
Structured dataTool / API callsJSON payloadPreciseRigid
Shared memoryShared storageVector storePersistentStale/noisy
Protocol messagesStandardized A2A layerJSON-RPC messageInteroperableStill text-bound
Environment stateIndirect coordinationTicket statusDecoupledWeak shared context



These coordination patterns are easy to inspect and orchestrate, but they also create a coordination tax. Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems shows how multi-agent variants experience degraded performance by 39 to 70% on sequential reasoning tasks. CooperBench arrives at a complementary diagnosis from collaborative coding: agents often perform worse together than alone (curse of coordination), and failures cluster around broken expectations, broken commitments, and communication breakdowns. 

Taken together, these results suggest that coordination becomes increasingly difficult as agents must continuously maintain, exchange, and reconstruct context across multiple steps. This broader coordination problem has several dimensions, but one of the major bottlenecks appears at the communication layer.

At a deeper level, this points to a structural mismatch between how models process information and how they communicate. Models reason in high-dimensional hidden states (i.e., latent representations), but communication forces them to externalize that reasoning into tokens, exchange it, and reconstruct it again. In practice, this leads to repeated cycles of decode -> serialize -> re-tokenize -> re-encode across agents. This introduces overhead, increases latency, and creates opportunities for information loss and drift.

This naturally raises the question: can agents communicate in a more model-native way than repeatedly exchanging tokens? 

Latent Memory: Communicating Beyond Words

To understand why latent memory is gaining traction, it helps to look at how LLMs represent and encode information:

Tokens are discrete symbols with limited information per unit, requiring reasoning to be serialized into language before it can be shared. Latent representations, by contrast, are high-dimensional continuous vectors that can encode richer information and be reused directly within the model’s computation — closer to how the model already “thinks” internally (kind of like telepathy).

This difference becomes critical at the communication interface. In token-based systems, reasoning must be externalized, transmitted, and reconstructed at every step, introducing both overhead and opportunities for information loss. Latent approaches, in contrast, allow agents to pass their internal state directly, avoiding this repeated translation and preserving more of the underlying signal. In effect, tokens act as a lower-bandwidth interface between agents, while latent memory enables more direct, model-native communication.

Three core trends regarding latent memory are becoming increasingly relevant for practical applications:

  • Latent reasoning: Models can operate over continuous hidden states without emitting intermediate text (e.g., Coconut).
  • Inter-agent latent communication: Agents begin to share working memory directly. LatentMAS passes KV cache across agents, while ThoughtComm explores structured latent exchange.
  • KV-cache infrastructure: KV cache is increasingly treated as a reusable systems primitive. Advances in compression, quantization (e.g., TurboQuant), and cache reuse (e.g., LMCache) make latent memory more practical.

To get a sense of this in practice, @Akshat6 tested a setup that combines latent reasoning and inter-agent latent communication (inspired by LatentMAS) on the FinQA reasoning benchmark using Qwen3-8B on a NVIDIA A40 GPU: 

 


FinQA-Experiment: Using Latent Communication in a MAS Increases Accuracy and Efficiency Over Text-Based Coordination 

 


Compared to the same multi-agent setup communicating in text, this yielded ~5% higher accuracy and a 1.7× speedup, partly from avoiding repeated tokenization of intermediate reasoning. But these gains also come with significant constraints:

  • Limited observability: Most reasoning happens in latent space, making systems harder to inspect, debug, and audit compared to text-based traces.
  • Infrastructure constraints: Requires direct access to KV cache, which today effectively limits this to self-hosted setups and excludes standard API-based models.
  • Memory and transfer overhead: KV caches can become large, creating pressure on VRAM and increasing bandwidth requirements when transferring state between agents.

 

Nevertheless, we believe latent memory is a promising approach to reducing costs and latency in agent swarms. It can be used wherever in-depth auditability and explainability of individual reasoning loops are a second-order concern. 

 

Outlook and Open Questions 


Despite the potential of latent memory, practical applications are still in their infancy, and it is not yet clear what the full stack around it will look like. We see a whole ecosystem that could evolve: latent APIs for models that expose hidden state, shared latent working memory between agents, protocols for computational state transfer, latent checkpoints, latent debuggers, KV-cache compression to reduce transfer overhead, and so forth. 

Aside from these technical considerations, several foundational questions remain:

  • How can latent exchanges be made observable and auditable without relying entirely on text traces?
  • How stable is shared latent memory over longer workflows, especially across multiple handoffs and tool use?
  • When does latent collaboration actually outperform text-based systems in practice, and how should that be evaluated?
  • How well does latent collaboration generalize across different models, and as model size scales?

 

# CTO Research and Innovation Munich
@Akshat6 
@ladenaw

entry.title: How to Buffer Master Data in Sales Documents to Improve Performance
entry.content:
Overview

In this blog post, you'll learn how to buffer master data during sales document processing to avoid unnecessary CDS view or database calls. These calls can have a negative impact on API calls or apps. 

In our example, we use key user tools, but you could do this in a similar way also using developer extensibility.

 

Example

Consider a typical scenario: You want to read the Customer ABC classification from I_CustomerSalesArea for the ship-to party of each item in a sales order.

When you create a sales order, the Modification of Item Custom Fields in Sales Documents Business Add-In (BAdI SD_SLS_MODIFY_ITEM) is usually called three times per item. For a sales order with 1,000 items, that means 3,000 BAdI executions.

If your BAdI implementation reads master data from a CDS view in every single call, you hit the database thousands of times, even though the data you read is identical across many items.

In a sales order with 1,000 items and 5 different ship-to parties, the implementation reads the database up to 3,000 times, when 5 times would be enough.

The solution is buffering: Store the result of each unique lookup in the memory and reuse it on subsequent calls. You can achieve this with static buffering via a custom library (custom reusable element).

 

Implementation Concept

Custom reusable elements are reusable ABAP classes created in the Custom Reusable Elements app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
When you use a static attribute for the class, the buffer is kept for the entire duration of the Logical Unit of Work (LUW) and is shared across all BAdI calls within the same request.

The pattern is simple:

1. A static internal table holds results that were already read, keyed by the lookup key.
2. A static method checks the buffer first. If the entry exists, it returns it immediately. If not, it reads from the CDS view, stores the result in the buffer, and then returns it.

 

Implementation Steps

1. Create a Custom Class (Custom Reusable Element)

  1. In the Custom Reusable Elements app, create a new custom library:

    This generates a global ABAP class (in our example, YY1_BUFFER) with language version 5.

  2. Define a structure type for the customer sales area buffer as follows:

    Component IDTypeLength
    CUSTOMERText10
    SALESORGANIZATIONText4
    DISTRIBUTIONCHANNELText2
    DIVISIONText2
    ABCCLASSIFICATIONText2
     
  3. Define structures, tables, and data as follows:

     

  4. Define a new method GetABCClassification() with the following parameter:


  5. Implement the method as follows:
*Check internal buffer
Read table yy1_buffer=>abc_classifications with key
    customer = customer
    distributionchannel = distributionchannel
    division = division
    salesorganization = salesorganization
        assigning field-symbol(<abc_classification>).

IF sy-subrc = 0.
    ABCclassification = <abc_classification>-abcclassification.
    RETURN.
ENDIF.

" Cache miss — read from CDS view
SELECT SINGLE
CustomerABCClassification
FROM I_CustomerSalesArea
WHERE Customer = 
AND SalesOrganization = @salesorganization
AND DistributionChannel = @distributionchannel
AND Division = @division
INTO @abcclassification.

" Store in buffer — also stores empty result to avoid repeated failed lookups
INSERT value #(
customer = customer
salesorganization = salesorganization
distributionchannel = distributionchannel
division = division
abcclassification = abcclassification
) INTO TABLE yy1_buffer=>abc_classifications.
​

Note: The buffer also stores entries where no classification was found (empty value). This prevents repeated database hits for customers that simply have no ABC classification.

 

2. Implementation of BAdI SD_SLS_MODIFY_ITEM

Note: Prerequisite for this step is that you create a custom field. In the Custom Fields app, we created a custom field YY1_ABCCLASSIFICATION_SDI (type Text, Length 2) using business context Sales: Sales Document Item for our example.

Now we want to use the newly introduced buffer. In the Custom Logic app,  we create the following implementation for BAdI SD_SLS_MODIFY_ITEM :

*   Assign existing extension field values to output parameter:
salesdocumentitem_extension_o = salesdocumentitem_extension_i.

*GET ABC classification of ship-to party
*Check if there is an item-specific ship-to party
data(shipto) = value #(  salesdocumentitempartners[ salesdocumentitem = salesdocumentitem-salesdocumentitem
                                          partnerfunction = 'WE' ]-customer optional ).
if shipto is initial.
*Take ship-to party from header
    shipto = salesdocumentpartners[ 'WE' ]-customer.
endif.

*Get ABC classification of ship-to party using the buffered class
salesdocumentitem_extension_o-yy1_abcclassification_sdi = yy1_buffer=>getabcclassification(
                                                            customer            = shipto
                                                            division            = salesdocument-organizationdivision
                                                            salesorganization   = salesdocument-salesorganization
                                                            distributionchannel = salesdocument-distributionchannel
                                                          ).

 

Summary & Key Takeaways

Why This Works: The LUW Lifecycle

A custom library (static class) attribute is kept for the entire Logical Unit of Work (LUW), that is, the entire duration of a single API request or dialog transaction. Within one sales order save operation, all BAdI calls share the same buffer instance.

- Always buffer master data reads in item-level BAdIs because these BAdIs are called multiple times per item.
- Use custom libraries as buffers, they are persisted for the entire LUW at no extra cost.
- Buffer negative results, too (cases with no results found) to avoid repeated failed lookups.
- This pattern works for any kind of master data: customers, materials, plants, custom Z-tables, and so on.

 

Further Information

If you want to take it a step further, the ABC classification of the ship-to party written to YY1_ABCCLASSIFICATION_SDI can then be picked up by a pricing condition in your pricing procedure to provide additional discounts. The configuration of this pricing condition is outside the scope of this blog post. You can find further information about using custom fields to influence the price of a sales document in this blog post. If you're new to key user extensibility in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, I recommend that you start with this blog post.

entry.title: Activating Joule for Developers (J4D) – BTP Trust Store Certificate Configuration in STRUST
entry.content:
This blog is a continuation of the series on activating Joule for Developers (J4D) with ABAP AI capabilities for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025. It focuses specifically on the BTP Trust Store Certificate installation step — uploading the required .pem certificates into the SSL Client created during the destination certificate setup.

Prerequisites

Before following the steps in this blog, ensure you have completed the destination certificate configuration described in the blog below. A new SSL Client (e.g., J4DFIN) must already be created with the destination certificate in place.

  • Destination certificate configured and SSL Client created — Generate Client Certificate for Joule for Developers: ABAP AI Capabilities
  • Access to transaction code STRUST (Trust Manager) in the SAP system
  • Access to transaction code SMICM (ICM Monitor) for the soft restart
  • All required .pem certificates downloaded from the BTP Trust Store repository on GitHub

Background

For Joule for Developers to communicate securely with SAP BTP services, the SAP system must trust the certificate authorities used by BTP. These root and intermediate CA certificates are maintained in the official SAP BTP Trust Store repository on GitHub:

https://github.com/sap-software/btp-trust-store/tree/main/required/pem

All certificates listed under the /required/pem path must be imported into the SSL Client in STRUST so that outbound TLS connections from the ABAP system to BTP endpoints are established successfully.

Step-by-Step: Installing BTP Trust Store Certificates

Step 1 — Download the Required Certificates

Navigate to the BTP Trust Store GitHub repository and download all .pem certificate files from the /required/pem folder. These include DigiCert, SAP, Entrust, and ISRG root certificates.

Step 2 — Open STRUST and Locate the SSL Client

Open transaction STRUST in the SAP system. In the navigation tree on the left, locate and double-click the SSL Client that was created in the prerequisite step (e.g., SSL client J4D forABAP Test Subaccount or J4DFIN). The panel on the right will display the Own Certificate section, confirming the destination certificate is in place.

Figure 1: Trust Manager (STRUST) – SSL Client with Own Certificate (destination certificate) already configured

Step 3 — Import Each Certificate

Scroll down to the Certificate area. Click the menu path Certificate > Import (or use the Import icon highlighted below) to open the file selection dialog.

Figure 2: Click the Import icon (highlighted in red) to open the Import Certificate dialog

In the file dialog, navigate to the folder where you saved the downloaded .pem files. Select the first certificate file and click Open.

Figure 3: File selection dialog showing the BTP Trust Store .pem certificates (DigiCert, Entrust, ISRG, SAP roots)

Step 4 — Add the Certificate to the Certificate List

After the file is loaded, the certificate details (Subject, Issuer, Serial Number, Algorithm, validity dates) will appear in the Certificate panel in the lower right area of the screen. Verify that the certificate information is correctly displayed, then click the Add to Certificate List button.

Figure 4: Certificate details for DigiCert Global Root CA — click Add to Certificate List (highlighted in red) to include it
Important: Make sure the certificate information is still visible in the Certificate area before clicking Add to Certificate List. If the panel is blank, re-import the certificate.

Step 5 — Save and Repeat for All Remaining Certificates

Click Save (or press Ctrl+S). Then repeat Steps 3 to 5 for each of the remaining .pem certificate files. Once all certificates have been added, the Certificate List section will display all imported CA entries with their validity dates.

Figure 5: Certificate List populated with all required BTP Trust Store certificates (DigiCert, SAP Cloud Root CA, ISRG Root X1, SAP Global Root CA)

Step 6 — Perform a Soft Restart via SMICM

After all certificates are loaded and saved, the ICM (Internet Communication Manager) must be restarted for the changes to take effect. Open transaction SMICM and perform a Soft Restart (Global):

1. Open transaction SMICM.
2. Navigate to the menu path: Administration > ICM > Exit Soft > Global.
3. Confirm the soft restart. The ICM will reload the SSL configuration, including the newly imported  certificates.

Next Steps

Once the certificates are loaded and the ICM has been restarted, continue with the J4D activation by following the instructions in the blog below:

SAP Joule for Developers: ABAP AI Capabilities for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

SAP Note 3627794
In case the program required for activating the client certificate is missing in your system, implement SAP Note 3627794 to apply the necessary correction.

entry.title: Re: PO net amount not shown in order currency in monitor...
entry.content:
Dear @Sudharmv,

SAP standard logic for Monitor Purchase Order Items is based on a display currency concept and SAP explicitly states that this app uses the exchange rate from Currency Exchange Rates for the PurchaseOrderDate with exchange rate type M. SAP also states that the exchange rate stored on the purchase order itself is not taken into account because of that design the app does not support showing Purchase Order Net Amount in each PO own order currency within the same result list

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3681306

https://help.sap.com/doc/7c9e0bbbd1664c2581b2038a1c7ae4b3/2602.500/en-US/FSD_CE2602.pdf

The SAP-supported workaround is to cover this requirement with a custom analytical query/report. SAP documents that Custom Analytical Queries can be used to create reporting from delivered business data, and the Purchase Order Item source contains the measure Net order value in purchase order currency. This is the clean Public Cloud approach if the business needs the value shown in the original currency of each purchase order

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/33192c1211354eed9e40fac66e55fcc1.html

Summary:

In standard Monitor Purchase Order Items the values are shown in the selected display currency, not per-PO order currency. If per-PO currency display is mandatory the requirement should be handled through a custom analytical query/report

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Settlement through report - RKO7KO8G
entry.content: Work orders will be settled through report RKO7KO8G (Actual Settlement: Internal Orders) only when they meet certain conditions like the order must be technically complete or closed, have costs to settle, and a valid settlement rule pointing to a receiver such as a cost center, WBS element, asset, or profitability segment. If these prerequisites are not met, the settlement run will skip the order.

entry.title: SuccessFactors Release 1H 2026, Important notes on EC, Platform, Time Management, my view
entry.content:
First thing First 😊. Screenshots have been taken from SAP release notes only.

Latest Home Page Is Now Automatically On by Default (WEF-167105) – As per 1H 2026 release SAP has enabled the latest home page experience automatically in all systems. In case if you don’t want to adopt the latest home page make sure after release hits your production system you are turning it off in the configuration.

Deprecation of Legacy Home Page Experience (WEF-166700) – Legacy Home will be deleted on Nov 13, 2026. Customer must plan accordingly to use the latest home page. Change Management team should communicate to the end users about that. In case if you are using automated test scripts then you need plan to update the test scripts as the screen is getting changed.

Deprecation of Legacy People Profile (SFEDU-21006)– Legacy people profile will be deleted on May 15, 2026. In the meantime, latest people profile will be universally available for all customers and does not require an enablement setting. As per my testing I can see there is no major issue in the latest people profile but for Time Off Solution if the “Time Account” object is unsecured then you have to adjust a bit the configuration of Time Account Object & few changes in RBP. SAP KBA for that KBA - 3625336.

Improvements to Enhanced People Search (ISH-28758) – It’s cool. User status can be used in the filter criteria. To filter, enter keywords such as inactive David or Active David. Also, for national id You can enter the asterisk (*) as a wildcard in the National ID field for the search criteria.

Enhancements to Editing UI of Full Profile (ECT-263295) – Few screens have been modified for latest people profile along with some error message layouts have been updated.

Important observation - There is no pie chart in Compensation Information.

 

Subheading Names for the Message Popup 

Direct Workflow Approval Link in Manage Data Page (MDF-89756) – In the Manage data page workflow approval link is available which helps streamlining the process of reviewing pending or completed workflows.

Auto-Reassigning Primary Phone and Email Records (ECT-254499) - After enabling the auto-reassignment option, when you import a new primary record, the system automatically updates the existing primary record to non-primary. The import is successful and a warning message is displayed.

Old version: It showed error message.

Automated Deletion of Future-Dated Job and Compensation Information Records After Employment Termination (ECT-260432) – If this feature is enabled then all future-dated Job Information records with the relevant event reason set to delete are automatically removed during the Terminate action, regardless of how the records were created.

In the earlier version, even after the termination the future dated Job & Compensation Information records remained in the system after the termination which required manual deletion.

Workflow Participant Information Available in Mobile Apps (MOB-107874) - Cool one. In the mobile app user can view the with whom the request is pending for approval. The feature applies to Employee Central, MDF, Time Off, and Time Sheet workflows.

 

 

Enhancements to Time Off and Time Sheet Custom Workflow Notifications (TIM-52812) – Nice one. Custom workflow notification text can be used for Time Off, Time Sheet and Single Record Approvals through the document generation mechanism. Following dynamic fields are supported.

  • Employee name
  • Time type recorded by the employee
  • Start Time/Start Date and End Time/End Date of the absence or attendance request

entry.title: Stock Allocation from Multiple MRP Area
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

 

We have two MRP areas within same plant. One for sales processing with Plant 1000 as MRP area and Second MRP area (Plant 1000 & Location : XYDM) for Production orders where business can run MRP for independent requirements and continuously manufacture Make To Stock Products. Business will transfer the stock from XYDM location to other storage locations within 1000 plant regularly. 

During Sales order creation, availability will be performed within MRP area 1000 & stock available other than XYDM storage location will be reserved.  Business wants the automatic stock allocation from other MRP area also if stock not available in the main MRP area. The Products are batch managed. Recommended the customer to add the storage location in the Sales order so that the stock from XYDM location can be considered. Business team not accepting manual intervention and expecting auto allocation.

Customer using S4 HANA Public Cloud system and Basic ATP enabled. Customer not ready to spend for aATP.

entry.title: Customizing GRC NWBC screens globally without PFCG role update
entry.content:
Challenge: Fragmented GRC NWBC appearance and work center structure for different user groups in organisation leading to inconsistent UI layouts when accessing GRC functionality through NWBC, leading to reduced productivity and increased training costs. A uniform NWBC Landing page need to be established.

 

Implementation Approach: A uniform NWBC Landing page need to be configured which is applicable across all users accessing NWBC launch screen without changing roles.

In this blog, the detailed steps for configuring NWBC screens in highlighted.

Benefits

  • Eliminates Need for Technical ABAP Changes

Many minor UI adjustments, such as showing/hiding: Hyperlinks, Menu sections, Application nodes can be handled via customizing mode without involving developers

  • Helps Maintain Audit & Compliance Standards

Since NWBC is used by auditors, risk teams, and provisioning teams, controlling UI visibility helps ensure Least privilege navigation, No exposure of admin tools to business users, Clear segmentation of duties within the UI.

 

Environment: GRC Access Control 12.0

 

To achieve Centralized Control of UI Structure, GRC Admins can change what appears on NWBC for all users, from a single configuration point. No need to adjust individual roles repeatedly.

 In NWBC landing page, Right click -> option ‘Call Application in customizing mode’.

 

Segregation of Duties approach to prevent unintended customization:

Authorization object S_WDR_ADM controls the access to option ‘Call Application in customizing mode’ through which elements like visibility of each hyperlink/section can be globally changed for all users.

  • GRC Admins (Super user) or User designated to perform NWBC UI changes can be assigned with Authorization object S_WDR_ADM with full access in the PFCG role
  • For all other users, restrict Authorization object S_WDR_ADM to display only or disable it in the PFCG role.

 

Steps:

NOTE:  Exercise caution in applying these changes as it will be applicable to all users accessing NWBC irrespective of role assignment.

For Example - If there is a requirement that hyperlink ‘Work Inbox – Simplified’ should not be visible to any users who uses NWBC, then we can follow the below steps

1. Right Click on hyperlink ‘Work Inbox – Simplified’ -> Call Application in Customizing mode

2. Choose the Transport Request

3. Right click -> Call Application in Classic Customizing Mode

4. Right click -> Settings for Current Configuration

5. Set the Visibility to Invisible. 

6. After the changes are applied, ‘Work Inbox – Simplified’ hyperlink is not visible. You can always switch it back to default by following the same previous steps

 

You can follow the same steps to customise other NWBC screen hyperlinks or buttons.

 

Conclusion:

You have now successfully customised the GRC NWBC screen to display only required hyperlinks for all users.

entry.title: Re: Best Practices for Mining Industry - SAP S/4HANA Publ...
entry.content:
Dear @melikegenisler,

This recommendation is tailored to the Mining industry from a project-approach perspective. SAP current Public Cloud positioning for mining is based on standard best-practice processes and industry guidance, not on a separate mining-specific scope package or dedicated scope items strictly for the Mining industry. Therefore the safest approach is to start from the core standard scope in SAP Signavio Process Navigator and then assess mining-specific requirements separately

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/exploring-end-to-end-business-processes-in-sap-business-suite/navigating-the-sap-best-practice-solution-processes-via-sap-signavio-process-navigator

As a starting point I would recommend focusing first on:

  • Record-to-Report / finance foundation: J58 Accounting and Financial Close; optionally 1GA Accounting and Financial Close Group Ledger IFRS if an additional IFRS ledger is required
  • Procure-to-Pay: J45 Procurement of Direct Materials, BNX Consumable Purchasing and 22Z Procurement of Services
  • Enterprise Asset Management / maintenance: 4HH Reactive Maintenance and 4HI Proactive Maintenance if relevant also 43R Maintenance Resource Scheduling
  • Projects: 1NT Project Control for Finance supported by 1YF Review a Financial Project
  • Inventory and warehouse visibility: 3BR Warehouse Inbound Processing and 3BS Warehouse Outbound Processing

After that mining-specific gaps such as weighbridge integration, commodity/commercial requirements, transport integration, lab/quality or site-specific field operations should be assessed separately and where needed addressed through clean-core extensions or partner solutions rather than ERP-heavy modification

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Unified Role-Based Compliance & Skills: Bridging the Gap Between Learning and Talent Intelligence Hu
entry.content:
In highly regulated industries compliance isn't just a "nice-to-have"; it is the backbone of operations. However, as we transition into the era of the Skills-Based Organization, many of us are encountering a significant architectural hurdle: How do we create a single source of truth for qualifications that satisfies both the Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) and the rigorous tracking of SuccessFactors Learning (LMS)?

I am currently working on a scenario where the goal is to harmonize job-specific qualifications, recurring certifications, and the new Growth Portfolio.

The Business Challenge: Fragmented Data & Administrative Overhead
Currently, many organizations face a "double maintenance" trap. Job requirements are defined in the Job Profile Builder (JPB), while the actual mandatory training and re-certification logic (e.g., annual safety instructions) live within the LMS.

This creates several pain points:

Inconsistent Data: A "skill" in TIH might not reflect a "certification" status in Learning.

Manual Mapping: Administrators must manually ensure that a specific job role is assigned the correct curricula in the LMS.

Lack of Transparency: Managers often have to jump between the Growth Portfolio and LMS Reports to see if their team is truly "ready to work."

Our Proposed Solution Concept
To solve this, we are looking at a framework that centers on the Job Architecture as the driver for compliance:

Job Profile Builder as the Anchor: Define all role-specific requirements (one-time and recurring) directly within the Job Role.

TIH as the Central Hub: Use the Talent Intelligence Hub to aggregate these attributes. We want to see "Certificates" as a specific attribute type that mirrors LMS completion statuses.

Automated Sync: Establishing a bidirectional flow where an LMS completion automatically updates the Growth Portfolio and validates the requirement in the Job Profile.

Centralized Reporting: Utilizing the Report Center to build dashboards that merge TIH skill data with LMS compliance deadlines.

The "Golden Grail": Role-Based Automation
The ultimate goal is to reduce administrative effort. Imagine a world where you don't assign a "Safety Training" element in the LMS, but rather define a "Safety Requirement" on the Job Role. SuccessFactors would then automatically trigger the corresponding Learning Path and update the employee's Skill Profile upon completion.

Questions to the Community | Seeking Best Practices
We believe this integration is crucial for the long-term adoption of the Talent Intelligence Hub. However, we would love to hear from other implementation partners and customers:

How are you handling recurring compliance? Are you using TIH "Attributes" to track validity periods, or are you keeping that logic strictly within the LMS?

Data Consistency: Have you found a sustainable way to prevent "data silos" between the Growth Portfolio and the LMS history?

Talent Intelligence Hub Mapping: Which attribute types are you using in TIH to represent formal licenses versus soft skills?

Does this resonate with your project? We believe many companies would benefit from a more native, role-based compliance logic within the SF Suite. If you have implemented a similar "integrated" approach or have thoughts on the upcoming SAP roadmap regarding this, let's start a conversation in the comments!

entry.title: First Half 2026 Release: Agentic AI in SAP SuccessFactors Learning
entry.content:
At SAP SuccessFactors, we’re focused on turning learning, career, and talent development into a strategic engine that helps people grow where business needs them most. By embedding skills-first development and data-driven talent planning into the flow of work, we’re making upskilling continuous, actionable, and aligned to future demands—so employees progress faster and leaders can plan with confidence.

We are continuing to deliver innovations to create dynamic upskilling and continuous learning that supports growth and adaptability; build future-ready, agile skill capabilities that evolve with business needs; and enable strategic talent pipelines powered by predictive, data-driven development insights.

In 2025 we made learning, job, and opportunity recommendations more contextual and actionable. In 2026 we’re delivering several new enhancements that will help bring those capabilities to life with conversational and agent-driven experiences.

Learning with Intelligent Q&A

Learning with Intelligent Q&A allows users to utilize Joule to summarize content and link back to source material - providing employees quick answers and guided next steps without losing context. This new innovation enables your teams with greater efficiency in discovering the right answers to questions, a reduced need to navigate through an entire course to find the right reference, and improved individual and organizational learning outcomes; all of which greatly improve the employee experience.

Learning with Intelligent Q&A on the learning homepage

Learning Compliance Agent

The new Learning compliance agent uses AI to help support users to get their training completed on time, and assists managers with follow-up, reducing the administrative burden so employees and managers can focus on more strategic tasks. With AI-assisted nudges, users will stay compliant with guidance at every step as Joule will proactively monitor users’ compliance status, always ready to help them get back on track.

Learning Compliance agent on the learning homepage

Together, these updates reduce skill gaps, increase internal mobility, and make talent planning proactive rather than reactive—helping organizations keep pace with change while employees see clearer paths to growth.

Learning Insights in People Intelligence

Learning Insights dashboard page in People Intelligence

People Intelligence capabilities in SAP SuccessFactors Learning help organizations to understand learning impact and create a future-ready workforce by providing actionable insights into employee training, development, and skills acquisition across the organization.

Learning insights allows leaders to:

  • Identify Training Participation Trends: Tracks how many employees engage with learning activities and highlights demographic patterns, such as age groups most involved in training.
  • Measure Training Effectiveness: Assesses skills growth and the impact of training on key performance indicators like promotion rates and performance ratings.
  • Optimize Learning Investments: Pinpoints which courses are most popular and effective, guiding resource allocation toward high-impact learning programs.
  • Detect Skill Gaps: Reveals areas or groups with low training hours or limited skills growth, helping the business target future development efforts more effectively.
  • Support Compliance and Development Goals: Ensures training activities align with organizational goals for employee development, compliance, and workforce readiness.

Continuously Delivering Enhancements     

With every release, we are excited to deliver these new innovations and continue our journey of providing even greater value to our customers. For full details, please check out the 1H 2026 Release Highlights Resources: 

  • 1H 2026 Release Highlights Brochure
  • 1H 2026 Release Highlights Video 
  • Product Release & Road Map Information

Learn more about SAP SuccessFactors Learning.

entry.title: Re: Hierarchy upload from S/4 publich cloud to BW4H
entry.content:
Hello Guys,
we faced the same issues and finally got a solution at the end.
 
In Advance some information:
  • we solved it for S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. I cannot guarantee that it works for Public Edition as well!
  • we are using own InfoObject in BW, no 0GL_ACCOUNT etc
  • we are using Z-CDS Views, but those are using SAP CDS Views (names down below and in the mentioned blog)
 
Technical Steps:
1) Changing Data Model and InfoObject
First of all, we changed our own InfoObject for GL_Account plus the data model like described in the following blog (it is about Costcenter, but also worked for GL_Account):
https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/cds-based-data-extraction-part-iii-miscellaneous/ba-p/13452148
 
Some steps of the blog has to be adapt due to the fact that we loading GL_Account instead of Costcenter:
For the Hierarchy Node Text we used CDS View: I_GLAccountHierarchyNodeT
But we enhanced this CDS View with an association to table HRRP_NODET_N to add field KTOPL (Charts of Account). The Charts of Account is compounded to IO GL_Account in BW.
That means that we also compounded the extra InfoObject for the Hierarchy Node Text the blog writes about to the Charts of Account as well.
 
2) Implenting coding for Hierarchy-TRNF DS -> IO GL_Account
After all that we still had the issue Tom described.
His coding was a very good idea but it needs to be adjusted:
  • it needs to be in the startroutine
  • it has to write back the Fieldnames instead of the IO-names for Segment 0003
Like this:
    LOOP AT source_package_3 ASSIGNING <source_fields_3>.
      IF <source_fields_3>-fieldnm = 'HIERARCHYNODE'.
        <source_fields_3>-fieldnm = 'HIERARCHYNODE'.
      ELSE.
        <source_fields_3>-fieldnm = 'GLACCOUNT'.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.
If you are using the SAP CDS View I_GLAccountHierarchyNode (SQL View: IFIGLACCOUNTHN) for the hierarchy extraction, the coding should fit for you. If not you might have diffrent field names. Basically you have adress the coding to the fields which are used in the CDS View from table HRRP_NODE_N and fields HRYNODE + SAKNR.
 
3) Adjust rule in Hierarchy-TRNF DS -> IO GL_Account in Tab "Hierarchy Structure" (Segment 0003)
  • set the mapping of FIELDNM to OH_IOBJNM to Hierarchy Derivation
 
=== DONE ===
 
After all this the data load finally worked and the hierarchy in the query looks fine 🙂
 
Hope I didnt forget any important step and this helps to any who faces similar issues...

entry.title: Integration capabilities highly rated in SAP BTP reviews
entry.content:
Only a few short months ago I posted a blog in celebration of SAP Business Technology Platform's (SAP BTP) performance in G2’s Winter 2026 awards for their Cloud Platform as a Service (PaaS) category. Now having quickly accelerated to their Spring 2026 awards, once again I am happy to report that SAP BTP featured prominently, achieving leader badges along with a leaders position in the G2 Enterprise Grid for CPaaS.  

 Connections count!

Reading just a handful of the most recent SAP BTP reviews on the G2 website reveals just how important robust yet flexible integration is to SAP customers. Among the most recent customer reviews, many reveal Integration as something for which they rate SAP BTP highly:

  • 18 April 2026 I use SAP Business Technology Platform for data integration, analytics, and app development. It connects multiple systems, reduces data silos, and streamlines workflows, providing real-time insights. Its scalability handles large data efficiently. 
  • 16 April 2026 SAP Business Technology Platform helps address the challenge of integrating, extending, and modernising SAP landscapes by offering a unified platform for integration, analytics, data management, and application development. For us, this means simpler system integration, better visibility through analytics, and faster innovation without disrupting our core SAP systems. 
  • 10 April 2026 By utilizing the SAP Integration Suite, we successfully automated the data flow between our core ERP and third-party tools. The biggest benefit is the dramatic reduction in manual IT maintenance, saving us countless hours each month. 
  • 16 March 2026 SAP Business Technology Platform helps us connect systems, manage data, and build extensions for SAP apps without changing the core system, keeping it stable. It speeds up development by allowing integration and new functions, ensuring easy modifications without risking the main setup. 
  • 12 February 2026  SAP technology primarily solves the problem of disconnected, inefficient, and siloed business processes within organizations. By acting as a "central nervous system," SAP integrates data across finance.         

And this is just a small sample of the reviews available on G2 – there are many more that speak about the benefits of SAP BTP not only for integration, but application development, automation and support for clean core development too.

Springing forward with new badges

Having secured our Spring 2026 badges from G2, what next for SAP BTP? Obviously, Summer 2026 badges are now in view, and with support from our fabulous customers we shall hope to achieve these next. But first for the SAP BTP team, along with others from SAP, will be a focus on our very own Sapphire 2026 events, due to take place in Orlando and Madrid next month. The SAP BTP team will be presenting at both of these prestigious events, so if you happen to be joining one or the other, please make sure to come and say hello to the team on the show floor. And if you’re unable to join either of these events in-person, then that’s no problem, instead please join us at the SAP Sapphire Virtual event.

 With that I’ll sign off for this time, but meanwhile don’t forget to go to the G2 website and read more reviews for the customer-viewpoint on SAP BTP!

Chris Grundy, Product Marketing, SAP BTP
